preamble: Assibilation of [g] can lead to the voiceless sibilant [ʃ] (sh) through a chain of palatalization, voicing changes, and spirantization.

examples.note: Latin /g/ before front vowels → Old French [dʒ] → Modern French [ʒ]. NOTE: this example produces voiced [ʒ], not voiceless [ʃ]; the g→ʃ path would require additional devoicing.

In some Central Atlas Tamazight and Zenati Berber dialects, lax /g/ spirantizes through palatalization to the voiceless postalveolar fricative [ʃ]. This shift, documented by Kossmann (1997), is one of the most advanced outcomes of the Northern Berber spirantization chain (g → ɣ → ʝ → ʃ) and is particularly found in Zenati varieties of northern Algeria and Morocco.

sources: Kossmann, M. G. & Stroomer, H. J. (1997). Berber Phonology. In Kaye (ed.), Phonologies of Asia and Africa. Eisenbrauns.
